34	
35	You take **one high-effort blueprint declaration** and split it into a `\uses{}`-linked chain of **smaller, easier sub-lemmas**, so the prover formalizes a sequence of small goals instead of one large one. 36	
37	You decompose DOWN (one node → many smaller ones). This is the complement of the dag-walker (which fills the cone UP). You do not invent new mathematics — you re-express the target's existing proof as a chain of named intermediate results.

Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	\chapter{The tautological quotient and the universal property of $\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)$}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianQuot}
3	
4	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
5	
6	The previous chapter constructed the Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
7	\(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing the affine charts \(U^I\)
8	(\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) along the transition isomorphisms
9	(\cref{def:gr_transition}) satisfying the cocycle condition
10	(\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}), and proved that the structure morphism
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated})
12	and proper (\cref{lem:gr_proper}). This chapter adds the next layer: the
13	\emph{tautological rank-\(d\) quotient}
14	\(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of the
15	trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf, and the \emph{universal property} that makes
16	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) the fine moduli space of rank-\(d\) locally free quotients of
17	\(\mathcal{O}^{r}\) -- equivalently, the representing scheme of the Quot functor
18	of the trivial sheaf.
19	
20	Throughout, \(r \ge d \ge 1\) are the fixed integers of the previous chapter, and
21	for a size-\(d\) subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) we write \(R^I\) for the
22	chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) of \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) and \(R^I_J\) for the
23	localisation along \(P^I_J = \det(X^I_J)\) defining the overlap
24	\(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\).
25	
26	\section{Infrastructure: locally free sheaves and module gluing}
27	\label{sec:grquot_infra}
28	
29	The constructions of this chapter -- the universal quotient sheaf
30	\(\mathcal{U}\), the tautological quotient \(u\), and the functor of points -- rest
31	on two pieces of foundational machinery for sheaves of modules. The first is the
32	predicate ``locally free of rank \(d\)'' (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}),
33	which records that a sheaf of modules is, on the members of some open cover,
34	isomorphic to the trivial bundle \(\mathcal{O}^{d}\); it is the condition cut out
35	in the definition of the Grassmannian functor. That predicate is the project-local
36	\(\mathrm{SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank}\) already set up in the Quot-scheme
37	chapter (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}), which this chapter reuses verbatim;
38	the only foundational machinery genuinely new to this chapter is the second piece:
39	a gluing primitive that descends a sheaf of modules (and a morphism of such
40	sheaves) from per-chart data along an open cover, given a transition cocycle
41	compatible with the cover's own gluing; it is the vehicle that assembles the
42	per-chart free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}^{d}_{U^I}\) and the per-chart quotients
43	\(u^I\) into the global \(\mathcal{U}\) and \(u\), and is set up below.
44	
45	Stating the triple-overlap multiplicativity of the descent datum, however, first
46	requires a small piece of base-change machinery for the pullback functor on sheaves
47	of modules: the three transition isomorphisms attached to a triple of charts live a
48	priori on three different overlaps, and to compare their composite they must be
49	transported to a single common overlap. The next two blocks supply exactly this
50	transport -- the pseudofunctorial comparison of iterated pullbacks
51	(\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}) and its packaging along the triple-overlap data of a
52	scheme glue datum (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}) -- after which the
53	cocycle condition can be expressed cleanly.
54	
55	\begin{definition}[Comparison of iterated pullbacks of sheaves of modules]
56	  \label{def:modules_pullbackComp}
57	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}
58	  \mathlibok
59	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
60	  Let \(a : X \to Y\) and \(b : Y \to Z\) be morphisms of schemes. For a sheaf of
61	  \(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}\), pulling back first along \(b\) and then
62	  along \(a\) agrees with pulling back along the composite \(b \circ a\): there is a
63	  natural isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
64	  \[
65	    a^{*}\bigl(b^{*}\mathcal{M}\bigr)
66	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
67	    (b \circ a)^{*}\mathcal{M},
68	  \]
69	  natural in \(\mathcal{M}\), expressing that \(\mathcal{M} \mapsto \mathcal{M}\)'s
70	  pullback is a pseudofunctor on schemes. These comparison isomorphisms satisfy the
71	  pentagon (associativity) coherence for a triple of composable morphisms. This is the
72	  pullback half of the pseudofunctoriality of sheaves of modules and is supplied by
73	  Mathlib.
74	\end{definition}
75	
76	\begin{lemma}\leanok
77	[Base-change transport of a transition isomorphism to a triple overlap]
78	  \label{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}
79	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackBaseChangeTransport}
80	  \uses{def:modules_pullbackComp, def:gr_the_glue_data}
81	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) with charts
82	  \(U_i\), overlaps \(V_{ij}\), overlap immersions \(f_{ij} : V_{ij} \hookrightarrow
83	  U_i\), transitions \(t_{ij} : V_{ij} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{ji}\), and triple-overlap
84	  data \(t'_{ijk}\) with its compatibility identity \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\). For an index
85	  triple \((i,j,k)\) write
86	  \[
87	    V_{ijk} \;=\; V_{ij} \times_{U_i} V_{ik}
88	  \]
89	  for the common triple overlap, with the two projections
90	  \(p^{ij}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ij}\) and \(p^{ik}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ik}\), and
91	  let \(t'_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{jki}\) be the glue datum's triple
92	  transition, satisfying
93	  \[
94	    t'_{ijk} \,\circ\, p^{jk}_{jki} \;=\; p^{ij}_{ijk} \,\circ\, t_{ij}
95	    \qquad (t_{\mathrm{fac}}).
96	  \]
97	  Given per-chart sheaves of modules \(\mathcal{M}_i\) and a transition isomorphism
98	  \[
99	    g_{ij} \;:\; f_{ij}^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
100	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
101	    (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j
102	  \]
103	  on the overlap \(V_{ij}\) (as in \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}), the
104	  \emph{base-change transport} of \(g_{ij}\) to the triple overlap \(V_{ijk}\) is the
105	  isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_{V_{ijk}}\)-modules
106	  \[
107	    \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}
108	      \;:\;
109	    (f_{ij} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
110	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
111	    (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j
112	  \]
113	  obtained by pulling \(g_{ij}\) back along \(p^{ij}_{ijk}\) and reassociating the
114	  iterated pullbacks via \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. With the analogous transports
115	  \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) and \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) (the first taken along
116	  \(t'_{ijk}\), so that \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) identifies its source and target sheaves
117	  with those of \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) and \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\)), all three
118	  transported isomorphisms become endomorphism-composable: \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\)
119	  and \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) land on a common sheaf, and their composite is
120	  comparable to \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) on \(V_{ijk}\).
121	\end{lemma}
122	
123	\begin{proof}
124	  \uses{def:modules_pullbackComp, def:gr_the_glue_data}
125	  The pullback functor on sheaves of modules is a pseudofunctor: for composable
126	  scheme morphisms it carries the comparison isomorphisms of
127	  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}, natural in the sheaf and coherent for triple
128	  composites. Pulling the transition isomorphism \(g_{ij}\) back along the projection
129	  \(p^{ij}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ij}\) yields an isomorphism between
130	  \((p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*} f_{ij}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) and \((p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}(t_{ij} \circ
131	  f_{ji})^{*}\mathcal{M}_j\); applying \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} to each side
132	  reassociates these into the single-pullback form
133	  \((f_{ij} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) and
134	  \((t_{ij} \circ f_{ji} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\mathcal{M}_j\), giving
135	  \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\). For the \((j,k)\)-transition the same construction is
136	  applied along the triple transition \(t'_{ijk}\) rather than a bare projection; the
137	  glue datum's compatibility identity \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\),
138	  \(t'_{ijk} \circ p^{jk}_{jki} = p^{ij}_{ijk} \circ t_{ij}\), guarantees that the
139	  source of \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) coincides with the target of
140	  \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) (both are the pullback of \(\mathcal{M}_j\) along the same
141	  morphism \(V_{ijk} \to U_j\)), so the two are composable as endomorphism-style
142	  isomorphisms over \(V_{ijk}\). The reassociations introduced by
143	  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} are coherent (they satisfy the pentagon identity), so
144	  the composite \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i} \circ \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) and the third
145	  transport \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) are isomorphisms between the same pair of
146	  sheaves on \(V_{ijk}\), and may be compared directly. This is the module-level
147	  analogue of the scheme-level triangle \(t'_{ijk}\)/\(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) and is the
148	  shape in which the multiplicative cocycle condition is stated below.
149	\end{proof}
150	
151	\begin{lemma}[Triple-overlap endpoint bridges]
152	  \label{lem:gr_glueData_bridges}
153	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_src,
154	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_mid,
155	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_tgt}
156	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data}
157	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) and fix
158	  indices \(i,j,k\) with triple overlap \(V_{ijk} = V_{ij} \times_{U_i} V_{ik}\).
159	  The three transports of \cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport} have
160	  endpoints pinned down by three scheme-morphism equalities on \(V_{ijk}\), each a
161	  pure consequence of the glue datum's structural identities:
162	  \begin{itemize}
163	    \item (\emph{src}) \(\mathrm{fst} \circ f_{ij} = \mathrm{snd} \circ f_{ik}\)
164	      -- the defining pullback condition of \(V_{ijk}\);
165	    \item (\emph{mid}) \(\mathrm{fst} \circ (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})
166	      = (t'_{ijk} \circ \mathrm{fst}) \circ f_{jk}\) -- the pullback condition
167	      composed with the glue datum's \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) identity;
168	    \item (\emph{tgt}) \((t'_{ijk} \circ \mathrm{fst}) \circ (t_{jk} \circ f_{kj})
169	      = \mathrm{snd} \circ (t_{ik} \circ f_{ki})\) -- the cocycle core, from the
170	      \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) associativity, the reversed pullback condition, the
171	      \(t_{\mathrm{inv}}\) collapse and the glue datum's cocycle identity.
172	  \end{itemize}
173	  These three equalities align the source, middle, and target sheaves of the three
174	  transports so that the cocycle equation (C2) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}
175	  typechecks.
176	\end{lemma}
177	\begin{proof}
178	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data}
179	  Each equality is an identity of morphisms in the category of schemes, proved by
180	  rewriting with the glue datum's axioms: \emph{src} is literally
181	  \(\mathrm{pullback.condition}\); \emph{mid} adds the \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\)
182	  factorisation \(D.t_{\mathrm{fac}}\,i\,j\,k\); \emph{tgt} chains
183	  \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\)-associativity at \((j,k,i)\), the reversed pullback
184	  condition, an auxiliary collapse combining \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\)-associativity at
185	  \((k,i,j)\) with \(t_{\mathrm{inv}}\), and finally the cocycle identity
186	  \(D.\mathrm{cocycle}\,i\,j\,k\). No sheaf-of-modules data enters; these are
187	  bookkeeping equalities of the underlying scheme cover.
188	\end{proof}
189	
190	\begin{definition}
191	
192	\leanok
193	[Gluing a sheaf of modules along an open cover]
194	  \label{def:scheme_modules_glue}
195	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glue}
196	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data, def:modules_pullbackComp,
197	    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}
198	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}), with index set \(J\), charts \(U_i\),
199	  pairwise overlaps \(V_{ij}\), open immersions
200	  \(f_{ij} : V_{ij} \hookrightarrow U_i\), and transition isomorphisms
201	  \(t_{ij} : V_{ij} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{ji}\) satisfying the glue datum's
202	  cocycle conditions; write \(X\) for the glued scheme and
203	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\) for the canonical open immersions of the
204	  charts. Suppose given:
205	  \begin{itemize}
206	    \item for each \(i \in J\), a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}_i\) on the chart \(U_i\);
207	    \item for each pair \((i,j)\), a \emph{transition isomorphism} of sheaves of
208	      modules on the overlap \(V_{ij}\),
209	      \[
210	        g_{ij} \;:\; f_{ij}^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
211	          \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
212	        (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j,
213	      \]
214	      comparing the two charts' sheaves after restriction (pullback) to the
215	      common overlap \(V_{ij}\);
216	  \end{itemize}
217	  \emph{subject to the following module cocycle conditions}, compatible with the
218	  scheme-level cocycle of \(D\) (its triple transition data \(t_{ij}\) and the
219	  overlap immersions \(f_{ij}\)), which are required as hypotheses on the family
220	  \((g_{ij})\):
221	  \begin{enumerate}
222	    \item[(C1)] \emph{self-identity:} the self-transition is the identity,
223	      \(g_{ii} = \mathrm{id}\) on \(f_{ii}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) (with
224	      \(V_{ii}\) and \(t_{ii}\) the diagonal data of \(D\));
225	    \item[(C2)] \emph{triple-overlap multiplicativity:} over each common triple
226	      overlap \(V_{ijk} = V_{ij} \times_{U_i} V_{ik}\) the base-change transports
227	      (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}) of the three transition
228	      isomorphisms satisfy
229	      \[
230	        \widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i} \circ \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}
231	          \;=\; \widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j},
232	      \]
233	      where \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\), \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\),
234	      \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) are the transports of \(g_{ij}\), \(g_{jk}\),
235	      \(g_{ik}\) to \(V_{ijk}\) through the scheme-level triple transition
236	      \(t'_{ijk}\) and its compatibility \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\), reassociated by the
237	      pullback comparison \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} so that all three land on
238	      the same sheaf -- the multiplicative, \(\mathrm{GL}_d\)-valued cocycle
239	      condition.
240	  \end{enumerate}
241	  Conditions (C1) and (C2) are exactly the descent datum for the Zariski cover
242	  \(\{\iota_i\}\); it is subject to them that the gluing below is well-defined and
243	  the glued sheaf is characterised up to unique isomorphism. Out of this data the
244	  construction produces:
245	  \begin{enumerate}
246	    \item a glued sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}\) on the glued scheme \(X\); and
247	    \item for each \(i \in J\) a \emph{restriction isomorphism}
248	      \(\rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\,\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}_i\)
249	      identifying the pullback of \(\mathcal{M}\) along \(\iota_i\) with the
250	      \(i\)-th chart sheaf, the \(\rho_i\) being compatible with the transition
251	      isomorphisms \(g_{ij}\) over the overlaps.
252	  \end{enumerate}
253	  The pair \((\mathcal{M}, (\rho_i)_i)\) is characterised up to unique
254	  isomorphism by the restriction property: \(\mathcal{M}\) is the unique sheaf of
255	  modules on \(X\) (up to canonical isomorphism) restricting to each
256	  \(\mathcal{M}_i\) compatibly with \((g_{ij})\). The same primitive descends
257	  morphisms: given two such glued data \((\mathcal{M}_i, g_{ij})\) and
258	  \((\mathcal{N}_i, h_{ij})\) with glued sheaves \(\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}\), and
259	  a family of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-linear morphisms
260	  \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\) that commute with the transition
261	  isomorphisms on every overlap, there is a unique morphism
262	  \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) of sheaves of modules on \(X\) with
263	  \(\rho_i^{\mathcal{N}} \circ \iota_i^{*}\phi = \phi_i \circ \rho_i^{\mathcal{M}}\)
264	  for every \(i\) -- the descent of \(\mathrm{Hom}\) along the cover.
265	
266	  \emph{Construction.} A sheaf of modules on \(X\) is equivalent to a compatible
267	  family of sheaves on the members of an open cover of \(X\) together with
268	  identifications on the overlaps -- effective descent for the Zariski cover
269	  \(\{\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\}\). The charts \(U_i\), being the images of
270	  the glue datum's objects, form such a cover, with intersections
271	  \(U_i \cap U_j\) identified with the overlaps \(V_{ij}\) via the structural data
272	  of \(D\). Pulling the sections of an abstract \(\mathcal{M}\) back to each chart
273	  must reproduce \(\mathcal{M}_i\); the transition isomorphisms \(g_{ij}\),
274	  satisfying the cocycle conditions, are exactly the descent datum that makes the
275	  family \((\mathcal{M}_i)\) glue, so the descent produces \(\mathcal{M}\) with
276	  the asserted restriction isomorphisms \(\rho_i\). The morphism statement is the
277	  descent of morphisms for the same cover: a morphism on \(X\) is a family of
278	  morphisms on the charts agreeing on overlaps, and the compatibility of the
279	  \(\phi_i\) with \((g_{ij}, h_{ij})\) is precisely the agreement condition,
280	  giving the unique glued \(\phi\). This specialises the general descent of sheaves
281	  of modules to the concrete open cover supplied by a scheme glue datum,
282	  and is the form in which the Grassmannian's tautological bundle and quotient are
283	  built.
284	
285	  \emph{Realisation (construction path).} Concretely, instantiate \(D\) as the
286	  Grassmannian glue datum (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}), with glued scheme
287	  \(X = \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) and its canonical open cover by the charts
288	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\). The glued sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\) is built from
289	  the per-chart sheaves \(\mathcal{M}_i\) and the overlap transition isomorphisms
290	  \(g_{ij}\) satisfying the module cocycle conditions, and is pinned down by the
291	  restriction isomorphisms \(\rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim}
292	  \mathcal{M}_i\) compatible with the \(g_{ij}\). For an open immersion
293	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\), the pullback \(\iota_i^{*}\) restricts sheaves
294	  of modules on \(X\) to sheaves of modules on \(U_i\). The transition isomorphisms
295	  \(g_{ij}\), forming a cocycle for this cover, constitute a descent datum; gluing the
296	  local sections yields \(\mathcal{M}\) together with the restriction isomorphisms
297	  \(\rho_i\), where both the assembly and the characterisation up to unique isomorphism
298	  rest on the locality of sections of a sheaf -- a compatible family of sections over
299	  the cover, agreeing on the overlaps, extends to a unique global section.
300	
301	  The morphism-descent half is the same locality argument applied to homomorphisms:
302	  a family \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\) commuting with the transition
303	  isomorphisms over every overlap is a compatible family of local morphisms, and the
304	  uniqueness of gluing of sections promotes it to a unique global
305	  \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) of sheaves of modules on \(X\) restricting
306	  to each \(\phi_i\) through the \(\rho_i\).
307	\end{definition}
308	
309	\subsection{Pullback of free sheaves along an arbitrary morphism}
310	\label{sec:grquot_pullback_free}
311	
312	The functor of points (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}) acts on morphisms by pullback, so
313	it needs the pullback of the trivial bundle to be again trivial --
314	\(\varphi^{*}\mathcal{O}_T^{\,\oplus I} \cong \mathcal{O}_{T'}^{\,\oplus I}\) -- for an
315	\emph{arbitrary} scheme morphism \(\varphi\). Mathlib's pullback-of-free comparison applies
316	only when the underlying site functor \(\mathrm{Opens.map}\,\varphi\) is \emph{final}; the
317	following three blocks remove that restriction for every morphism, and the resulting
318	pullback machinery preserves rank-\(d\) local freeness.
319	
320	\begin{lemma}\leanok
321	[The opens-pullback functor is final]
322	  \label{lem:gr_opensMap_final}
323	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.opensMap_final}
324	  For any morphism of schemes \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the inverse-image functor on opens
325	  \(\mathrm{Opens.map}\,\varphi : \mathrm{Opens}(T) \to \mathrm{Opens}(T')\),
326	  \(U \mapsto \varphi^{-1}U\), is \emph{final}.
327	\end{lemma}
328	
329	\begin{proof}
330	  Finality means that for every open \(V \subseteq T'\) the structured-arrow category of
331	  opens \(U \subseteq T\) with \(V \le \varphi^{-1}U\) is connected. That category is
332	  nonempty and has the terminal object \(U = \top\) -- one always has
333	  \(V \le \varphi^{-1}\top = T'\), and every object admits a unique arrow to \(\top\) --
334	  and a category with a terminal object is connected.
335	\end{proof}
336	
337	\begin{lemma}\leanok
338	[Pullback of a free sheaf is free]
339	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
340	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso}
341	  \uses{lem:gr_opensMap_final}
342	  For any scheme morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\) and index type \(I\), there is a canonical
343	  isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_{T'}\)-modules
344	  \[
345	    \varphi^{*}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,\oplus I}\bigr)
346	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
347	    \mathcal{O}_{T'}^{\,\oplus I}.
348	  \]
349	\end{lemma}
350	
351	\begin{proof}
352	  \uses{lem:gr_opensMap_final}
353	  Mathlib's pullback-of-free comparison produces this isomorphism whenever the site functor
354	  \(\mathrm{Opens.map}\,\varphi\) is final; that finality is \cref{lem:gr_opensMap_final},
355	  which holds for every morphism, so the comparison applies unconditionally.
356	\end{proof}
357	
358	\begin{lemma}\leanok
359	[Pullback preserves rank-\(d\) local freeness]
360	  \label{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank}
361	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank}
362	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:modules_pullbackComp}
363	  Let \(\varphi : T' \to T\) be a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{M}\) a sheaf of
364	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-modules locally free of rank \(d\)
365	  (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}). Then the pullback \(\varphi^{*}\mathcal{M}\) is
366	  locally free of rank \(d\) on \(T'\).
367	\end{lemma}
368	
369	\begin{proof}
370	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:modules_pullbackComp}
371	  Choose an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(T\) trivialising \(\mathcal{M}\),
372	  \(\mathcal{M}|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}^{\,d}\). The preimages
373	  \(\{\varphi^{-1}U_i\}\) cover \(T'\). On each member the factorisation
374	  \(\varphi \circ \iota_{\varphi^{-1}U_i} = (\varphi|_{U_i}) \circ \iota_{U_i}\) of the
375	  restricted morphism, together with the pseudofunctor comparison of iterated pullbacks
376	  (\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}), identifies
377	  \((\varphi^{*}\mathcal{M})|_{\varphi^{-1}U_i}\) with
378	  \((\varphi|_{U_i})^{*}\mathcal{O}_{U_i}^{\,d}\); the latter is
379	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\varphi^{-1}U_i}^{\,d}\) by \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}. Hence
380	  \(\varphi^{*}\mathcal{M}\) is locally free of rank \(d\).
381	\end{proof}

383	\section{The tautological quotient on the charts}
384	\label{sec:grquot_chart}
385	
386	\emph{Infrastructure: scalar endomorphisms.} Realising a matrix of regular
387	functions as a morphism of free sheaves of modules has no off-the-shelf primitive
388	in Mathlib; it is assembled from two small project-local helpers that turn a global
389	function into a scalar endomorphism of the structure sheaf, after which the matrix
390	is reconstituted entry-by-entry over a finite biproduct.
391	
392	\begin{definition}\leanok
393	[Global section of the unit module]
394	  \label{def:gr_globalUnitSection}
395	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.globalUnitSection}
396	  \uses{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
397	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) a global section of
398	  the structure sheaf. Viewing \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as the unit module
399	  \(\mathbf{1} \in \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}), the
400	  section \(a\) determines a global section of \(\mathbf{1}\): on each open
401	  \(V \subseteq X\) take the restriction \(a|_V \in \Gamma(V, \mathcal{O}_X)\), the
402	  family \((a|_V)_V\) being compatible with the restriction maps because
403	  restriction is functorial. This is the section of the unit sheaf attached to a
404	  global function.
405	\end{definition}
406	
407	\begin{definition}\leanok
408	[Scalar endomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)]
409	  \label{def:gr_scalarEnd}
410	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd}
411	  \uses{def:gr_globalUnitSection, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
412	  For \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) the \emph{scalar endomorphism}
413	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a) : \mathcal{O}_X \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is the
414	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear endomorphism of the unit module given by multiplication
415	  by \(a\). It is obtained from the global section of \cref{def:gr_globalUnitSection}
416	  under the canonical identification of endomorphisms of the unit module
417	  \(\mathbf{1}\) with its global sections, \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong
418	  \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\); concretely, on each open \(V\) it acts as
419	  multiplication by \(a|_V\). These scalar endomorphisms are the matrix-entry
420	  building blocks of the chart quotient.
421	\end{definition}
422	
423	\begin{lemma}\leanok
424	[Scalar endomorphism of the unit is the identity]
425	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_one}
426	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_one}
427	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
428	  The scalar endomorphism attached to the unit function \(1 \in
429	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) is the identity of the unit module:
430	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(1) = \mathrm{id}_{\mathbf{1}}\).
431	\end{lemma}
432	
433	\begin{proof}
434	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
435	  Multiplication by \(1\) is the identity on every section, so under the
436	  identification \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\) the section \(1\)
437	  corresponds to \(\mathrm{id}_{\mathbf{1}}\).
438	\end{proof}
439	
440	\begin{lemma}\leanok
441	[Scalar endomorphism of the zero function is zero]
442	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}
443	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_zero}
444	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
445	  The scalar endomorphism attached to the zero function \(0 \in
446	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) is the zero endomorphism:
447	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(0) = 0\).
448	\end{lemma}
449	
450	\begin{proof}
451	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
452	  Multiplication by \(0\) sends every section to \(0\), so the corresponding
453	  endomorphism of the unit module is the zero map.
454	\end{proof}
455	
456	\begin{definition}
457	
458	\leanok
459	[Chart quotient \(u^I\)]
460	  \label{def:gr_chart_quotient}
461	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap}
462	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd}
463	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
464	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
465	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L898-L910).
466	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
467	  % "We next define a rank $d$ locally free sheaf $\U$ on $\grass(r,d)$
468	  %  together with a surjective homomorphism $\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$.
469	  %  On each $U^I$ we define a surjective homomorphism
470	  %  $u^I :\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I} \to  \oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
471	  %  by the matrix $X^I$."
472	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
473	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). On the affine chart
474	  \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) define the
475	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}\)-linear homomorphism of trivial bundles
476	  \[
477	    u^I \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}
478	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}
479	  \]
480	  given by left multiplication by the universal \(d \times r\) matrix \(X^I\)
481	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}): on global sections it is the \(R^I\)-linear
482	  map \((R^I)^{r} \to (R^I)^{d}\), \(v \mapsto X^I v\). Since the \(I\)-th minor
483	  \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\) is the identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\), the composite of
484	  \(u^I\) with the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates
485	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \hookrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) is the
486	  identity; hence \(u^I\) is a split surjection of locally free sheaves, with
487	  target the free rank-\(d\) sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\).
488	
489	  \emph{Realisation.} The free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) and
490	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) are the finite biproducts (coproducts) of copies of
491	  the unit module \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_{U^I}\), indexed by \(\{1,\dots,r\}\)
492	  and \(\{1,\dots,d\}\). Each entry \(X^I_{p,q} \in R^I\) of the universal matrix
493	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) is a global function on \(U^I = \Spec R^I\),
494	  obtained from \(R^I\) through the canonical identification
495	  \(\Gamma(\Spec R^I, \mathcal{O}) \cong R^I\); the corresponding scalar
496	  endomorphism \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(X^I_{p,q}) : \mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\)
497	  (\cref{def:gr_scalarEnd}) is the \((p,q)\)-component of \(u^I\). Assembling these
498	  components over the index biproducts -- the universal property of the biproduct
499	  packaging a matrix of component morphisms into a single morphism of the free
500	  sheaves -- yields \(u^I\). This entry-by-entry assembly via the finite biproduct
501	  is the substitute for a (non-existent) Mathlib ``matrix \(\mapsto\) morphism of
502	  free sheaves'' primitive.
503	\end{definition}
504	
505	\begin{lemma}\leanok
506	[The $I$-columns of the chart quotient are the identity]
507	  \label{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_iFree}
508	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap_ιFree}
509	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd,
510	    lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero,
511	    lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
512	  For each \(k \in \{1,\dots,d\}\), the chart quotient \(u^I\)
513	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) carries the \(\iota_I(k)\)-th basis section of the
514	  free sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) -- where \(\iota_I(k)\) is the \(k\)-th
515	  element of \(I\) in increasing order -- to the \(k\)-th basis section of
516	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\); that is, the \(I\)-indexed columns of \(u^I\) form the
517	  identity:
518	  \[
519	    e_{\iota_I(k)} \,\circ\, u^I \;=\; e_k.
520	  \]
521	\end{lemma}
522	
523	\begin{proof}
524	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd,
525	    lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero,
526	    lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
527	  By the biproduct presentation of \(u^I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}), the
528	  composite \(e_{\iota_I(k)} \circ u^I\) reads off the \(\iota_I(k)\)-th column of the
529	  component matrix, whose \(p\)-th entry is the scalar endomorphism
530	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)})\). The \(I\)-indexed columns of the
531	  universal matrix \(X^I\), read through the order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \cong
532	  I\), form the \(d \times d\) identity (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}),
533	  so \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)} = \delta_{p,k}\). Hence the diagonal entry is
534	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(1) = \mathrm{id}\) (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_one}) and the
535	  off-diagonal entries are \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(0) = 0\)
536	  (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}); the resulting column is the \(k\)-th coordinate
537	  inclusion, giving \(e_{\iota_I(k)} \circ u^I = e_k\).
538	\end{proof}
539	
540	\begin{lemma}\leanok
541	[The chart quotient is an epimorphism]
542	  \label{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_epi}
543	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap_epi}
544	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient}
545	  The chart quotient \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
546	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) is an epimorphism of sheaves of modules.
547	\end{lemma}
548	
549	\begin{proof}
550	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_iFree}
551	  We exhibit a section, so that \(u^I\) is a \emph{split} epimorphism and hence an
552	  epimorphism. Let \(\iota_I : \{1,\dots,d\} \hookrightarrow \{1,\dots,r\}\) be the
553	  inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, \(k \mapsto \iota_I(k)\), the
554	  \(k\)-th element of \(I\) in increasing order. It induces a morphism of free
555	  sheaves \(s_I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\), the
556	  coordinate inclusion sending the \(k\)-th basis section to the
557	  \(\iota_I(k)\)-th. We claim
558	  \[
559	    u^I \circ s_I \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}}.
560	  \]
561	  Both sides are morphisms out of the free rank-\(d\) sheaf, which is the coproduct
562	  of \(d\) copies of the unit module; by the universal property of that coproduct
563	  it suffices to check the equality on each coordinate, i.e.\ to compare component
564	  matrices. The \((p,k)\)-component of \(u^I \circ s_I\) is the entry
565	  \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)}\) of the universal matrix at the \(I\)-indexed column, which
566	  is precisely the \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\). Since that minor is the
567	  \(d \times d\) identity (the columns of \(X^I\) indexed by \(I\), read through the
568	  order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \cong I\), form the identity block --
569	  \cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}), one has \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)} = \delta_{p,k}\),
570	  so \(u^I \circ s_I\) has the identity component matrix and equals
571	  \(\mathrm{id}\). Hence \(s_I\) splits \(u^I\) and \(u^I\) is a split
572	  epimorphism, in particular an epimorphism.
573	\end{proof}
574	
575	\begin{definition}
576	
577	\leanok
578	[The universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\)]
579	  \label{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}
580	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalQuotient}
581	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_transition,
582	    lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:scheme_modules_glue,
583	    lem:gr_glueData_bridges, def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
584	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
585	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
586	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L903-L910).
587	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
588	  % "Compatible with the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$
589	  %  for gluing the affine pieces $U^I$, we give gluing data
590	  %  $(g_{I,J})$ for gluing together the trivial bundles $\oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
591	  %  by putting
592	  %  $$g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I_J)$$"
593	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
594	  Over each overlap \(U^I_J = \Spec R^I_J\) define the bundle-transition matrix
595	  \[
596	    g_{I,J} \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} \;\in\; \mathrm{GL}_d(R^I_J),
597	  \]
598	  the Cramer inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}),
599	  which is invertible because \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\). The matrices
600	  \((g_{I,J})\) are compatible with the chart-gluing cocycle \((\theta_{I,J})\)
601	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{lem:gr_cocycle}): over a triple overlap they
602	  satisfy \(g_{I,K} = g_{J,K}\, g_{I,J}\) and \(g_{I,I} = 1\), the multiplicative
603	  cocycle condition for a vector bundle. Gluing the free rank-\(d\) sheaves
604	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) along the isomorphisms \(g_{I,J}\) over the cover
605	  \(\{U^I\}\) of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) therefore
606	  produces a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-module
607	  \(\mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). Its determinant line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\)
608	  has transition functions \(\det(g_{I,J}) = 1/P^I_J\), the data underlying the
609	  Pl\"ucker embedding.
610	\end{definition}

612	\begin{definition}
613	
614	\leanok
615	[The tautological quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}^{r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\)]
616	  \label{def:tautological_quotient}
617	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.tautologicalQuotient}
618	  \uses{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_chart_quotient,
619	    def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_cocycle,
620	    def:scheme_modules_glue}
621	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
622	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
623	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L908-L910).
624	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
625	  % "This is compatible with the homomorphisms $u^I$,
626	  %  so we get a surjective homomorphism
627	  %  $u : \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$."
628	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
629	  The chart quotients \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
630	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) are compatible with the bundle gluing
631	  \((g_{I,J})\) of \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}): over
632	  each overlap \(U^I_J\) one has the identity of \(d \times r\) matrices
633	  \[
634	    g_{I,J}\, X^I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I \;=\; X^J \qquad \text{on } U^I_J,
635	  \]

637	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}).
638	  Hence the local maps \(u^I\), transported through the trivialisations of
639	  \(\mathcal{U}\), agree on overlaps and glue to a single surjective
640	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-linear homomorphism
641	  \[
642	    u \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r}
643	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\; \mathcal{U},
644	  \]
645	  the \emph{tautological} (or \emph{universal}) rank-\(d\) quotient of the trivial
646	  rank-\(r\) sheaf. It is surjective because each \(u^I\) is (split) surjective and
647	  surjectivity is local on \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and \(\mathcal{U}\) is locally
648	  free of rank \(d\) by \cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}. The pair
649	  \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is the family of rank-\(d\) quotients carried
650	  by \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) itself.
651	\end{definition}
652	
653	\section{The functor of points}
654	\label{sec:grquot_functor}
655	
656	The Grassmannian functor sends a scheme \(T\) to the set of \emph{equivalence classes} of
657	rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). The following block packages a single such
658	quotient, the equivalence relation on them, and the pullback action through which the
659	functor acts on morphisms; the two coherence blocks after it isolate the comparison
660	identities that make that action functorial.
661	
662	\begin{definition}[Rank-\(d\) quotients and their pullback action]
663	  \label{def:gr_rankQuotient}
664	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient,
665	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.Rel,
666	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_refl,
667	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_symm,
668	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_trans,
669	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqSetoid,
670	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqPullback,
671	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqPullback_rel}
672	  \uses{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso,
673	        def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
674	  A \emph{rank-\(d\) quotient of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} on a scheme \(T\) is a triple
675	  \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-modules
676	  \(\mathcal{F}\) that is locally free of rank \(d\) (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank})
677	  together with an epimorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) out
678	  of the trivial rank-\(r\) bundle. Two such quotients \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\)
679	  and \(y = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) are \emph{equivalent}, written \(x \sim y\),
680	  when there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with
681	  \(q \circ f = q'\) (equivalently \(\ker q = \ker q'\)). This relation is reflexive
682	  (\(f = \mathrm{id}\)), symmetric (\(f \mapsto f^{-1}\)), and transitive (composition of the
683	  witnessing isomorphisms), hence an equivalence relation -- the setoid whose quotient is the
684	  functor's value at \(T\).
685	
686	  For a morphism \(\psi : T' \to T\) the \emph{pullback action} sends
687	  \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) to
688	  \[
689	    \psi^{*}x \;=\;
690	      \bigl\langle\, \psi^{*}\mathcal{F},\;
691	        \mathcal{O}_{T'}^{\,r} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
692	        \psi^{*}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\bigr) \xrightarrow{\ \psi^{*}q\ }
693	        \psi^{*}\mathcal{F} \,\bigr\rangle,
694	  \]
695	  re-presenting the source as the trivial bundle via the free-pullback isomorphism
696	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}. The result is again a rank-\(d\) quotient: the composite is
697	  an epimorphism because pullback, a left adjoint, preserves epimorphisms and the
698	  re-presentation is an isomorphism, and \(\psi^{*}\mathcal{F}\) is locally free of rank \(d\)
699	  by \cref{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank}. The pullback action respects the equivalence
700	  relation -- given \(f\) witnessing \(x \sim y\), the pulled-back isomorphism \(\psi^{*}f\)
701	  witnesses \(\psi^{*}x \sim \psi^{*}y\), by functoriality of pullback -- so it descends to a
702	  map of equivalence classes.
703	\end{definition}
704	
705	The two blocks below record the comparison identities, between the free-pullback isomorphism
706	\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} and Mathlib's pseudofunctor structure on pullback, that make
707	the descended pullback action of \cref{def:gr_rankQuotient} functorial.

715	  modules \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*}\mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\) -- the map underlying the
716	  free-pullback isomorphism \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} at a one-element index -- coincides
717	  with the component at \(\mathbf{1}\) of the pullback-identity isomorphism
718	  \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*} \cong \mathrm{id}\).
719	\end{lemma}

721	\begin{proof}
722	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
723	  The comparison morphism is the mate of \(\mathrm{id}_{\mathbf 1}\) under the
724	  pullback--pushforward adjunction: the image of the identity of \(\mathbf 1\) through the
725	  adjunction's hom-equivalence on the unit. For the identity scheme morphism the pushforward
726	  is the identity functor, so the adjunction unit is itself an identity; Mathlib's
727	  pullback-identity isomorphism is exactly the conjugate (left-adjoint transpose) of the
728	  pushforward-identity isomorphism. Tracing the identity through the hom-equivalence and the
729	  conjugate-transpose identity for the pullback-identity isomorphism shows the comparison
730	  equals the \(\mathbf 1\)-component of that isomorphism.
731	\end{proof}

\begin{definition}[The glued presheaf of modules: compatible families of chart sections]
  \label{def:gr_modules_gluePresheaf}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.gluePresheaf}
  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data, def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:over_restrict_pullback_iso}
  With the descent data of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue} -- a glue datum \(D\) with
  charts \(U_i\), overlaps \(V_{ij}\), immersions \(f_{ij}\) and transitions
  \(t_{ij}\), glued scheme \(X = D.\mathrm{glued}\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) with
  canonical immersions \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\), per-chart sheaves
  \(\mathcal{M}_i\) and transition isomorphisms \(g_{ij}\) -- define, for each open
  \(V \subseteq X\), a \emph{compatible family} over \(V\) to be a tuple
  \((s_i)_{i \in J}\) with \(s_i \in \Gamma(\mathcal{M}_i, \iota_i^{-1}V)\) for every
  \(i\), subject to the overlap relation: over each pairwise overlap \(V_{ij}\) the
  transition isomorphism \(g_{ij} : f_{ij}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i \xrightarrow{\sim}
  (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\mathcal{M}_j\) carries the restriction of \(s_i\) (read as a
  section of \(f_{ij}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) through the chart-restriction bridge
  \cref{lem:over_restrict_pullback_iso}) to the corresponding restriction of \(s_j\).
  The assignment
  \[
    V \;\longmapsto\; \bigl\{\,(s_i)_i \in \textstyle\prod_i \Gamma(\mathcal{M}_i,
      \iota_i^{-1}V) \;\big|\; \text{overlap-compatible via } (g_{ij})\,\bigr\},
  \]
  with restriction maps acting componentwise (\(s_i \mapsto s_i|_{\iota_i^{-1}W}\) for
  \(W \subseteq V\)), is a presheaf on \(X\): componentwise restriction preserves the
  overlap relation because restriction is functorial and each \(g_{ij}\) is natural.
  This is the underlying presheaf of the glued sheaf.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[The \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module structure on the glued presheaf]
  \label{def:gr_modules_gluePresheafModule}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.gluePresheafModule}
  \uses{def:gr_modules_gluePresheaf}
  The presheaf of \cref{def:gr_modules_gluePresheaf} is a presheaf of
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules: over each open \(V \subseteq X\) the set of compatible
  families is an \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\)-submodule of the product
  \(\prod_i \Gamma(\mathcal{M}_i, \iota_i^{-1}V)\), where \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\) acts on
  the \(i\)-th component through the restriction homomorphism
  \(\mathcal{O}_X(V) \to \mathcal{O}_{U_i}(\iota_i^{-1}V)\) induced by \(\iota_i\) and
  the \(\mathcal{M}_i\)-module structure. The overlap relation is preserved by addition
  and by scalar multiplication because each \(g_{ij}\) is \(\mathcal{O}\)-linear and the
  restriction-of-scalars maps commute with the chart immersions; the restriction maps
  of the presheaf are \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear for the same reason. This is the
  pointwise module structure assembled from the chart module structures.
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}[The glued presheaf is a sheaf]
  \label{lem:gr_modules_gluePresheaf_isSheaf}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.gluePresheafIsSheaf}
  \uses{def:gr_modules_gluePresheaf, def:gr_modules_gluePresheafModule,
    lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib, def:gr_glued_scheme}
  The presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules of
  \cref{def:gr_modules_gluePresheaf,def:gr_modules_gluePresheafModule} satisfies the
  sheaf condition: for any open \(V \subseteq X\) and any open cover
  \(\{V_\alpha\}\) of \(V\), a family of compatible families
  \((s^\alpha)_\alpha\) that agree on the pairwise intersections
  \(V_\alpha \cap V_\beta\) amalgamates to a unique compatible family over \(V\).
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:gr_modules_gluePresheaf, lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
  Gluing is checked componentwise. Fix a chart index \(i\); the \(i\)-th components
  \((s^\alpha_i)_\alpha\) form a compatible family of sections of the sheaf
  \(\mathcal{M}_i\) over the cover \(\{\iota_i^{-1}V_\alpha\}\) of \(\iota_i^{-1}V\)
  (their agreement on overlaps follows by restricting the hypothesis to the chart), so
  by the sheaf gluing axiom for \(\mathcal{M}_i\)
  (\cref{lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}) they amalgamate to a unique
  \(s_i \in \Gamma(\mathcal{M}_i, \iota_i^{-1}V)\). The tuple \((s_i)_i\) is again
  overlap-compatible: the defining relation via \(g_{ij}\) is an equality of sections
  over \(V_{ij}\), it holds after restricting to each \(\iota_i^{-1}V_\alpha\)
  (where it is the compatibility of \(s^\alpha\)), and equality of sheaf sections is
  local, so it holds over \(V_{ij}\). Uniqueness of the amalgamation is the separation
  half of the same sheaf condition applied componentwise. Hence the glued presheaf is a
  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
\end{proof}

213	      \]
214	      comparing the two charts' sheaves after restriction (pullback) to the

216	  \end{itemize}
217	  \emph{subject to the following module cocycle conditions}, compatible with the
218	  scheme-level cocycle of \(D\) (its triple transition data \(t_{ij}\) and the
219	  overlap immersions \(f_{ij}\)), which are required as hypotheses on the family
220	  \((g_{ij})\):

223	      \(g_{ii} = \mathrm{id}\) on \(f_{ii}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) (with
224	      \(V_{ii}\) and \(t_{ii}\) the diagonal data of \(D\));
225	    \item[(C2)] \emph{triple-overlap multiplicativity:} over each common triple
226	      overlap \(V_{ijk} = V_{ij} \times_{U_i} V_{ik}\) the base-change transports
227	      (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}) of the three transition
228	      isomorphisms satisfy
229	      \[

231	          \;=\; \widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j},
232	      \]
233	      where \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\), \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\),
234	      \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) are the transports of \(g_{ij}\), \(g_{jk}\),
235	      \(g_{ik}\) to \(V_{ijk}\) through the scheme-level triple transition
236	      \(t'_{ijk}\) and its compatibility \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\), reassociated by the
237	      pullback comparison \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} so that all three land on
238	      the same sheaf -- the multiplicative, \(\mathrm{GL}_d\)-valued cocycle
239	      condition.
240	  \end{enumerate}

243	  the glued sheaf is characterised up to unique isomorphism. Out of this data the

247	    \item for each \(i \in J\) a \emph{restriction isomorphism}

252	  \end{enumerate}

255	  modules on \(X\) (up to canonical isomorphism) restricting to each

261	  isomorphisms on every overlap, there is a unique morphism
262	  \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) of sheaves of modules on \(X\) with

266	  \emph{Construction.} A sheaf of modules on \(X\) is equivalent to a compatible
267	  family of sheaves on the members of an open cover of \(X\) together with
268	  identifications on the overlaps -- effective descent for the Zariski cover
269	  \(\{\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\}\). The charts \(U_i\), being the images of
270	  the glue datum's objects, form such a cover, with intersections
271	  \(U_i \cap U_j\) identified with the overlaps \(V_{ij}\) via the structural data
272	  of \(D\). Pulling the sections of an abstract \(\mathcal{M}\) back to each chart
273	  must reproduce \(\mathcal{M}_i\); the transition isomorphisms \(g_{ij}\),
274	  satisfying the cocycle conditions, are exactly the descent datum that makes the
275	  family \((\mathcal{M}_i)\) glue, so the descent produces \(\mathcal{M}\) with
276	  the asserted restriction isomorphisms \(\rho_i\). The morphism statement is the
277	  descent of morphisms for the same cover: a morphism on \(X\) is a family of
278	  morphisms on the charts agreeing on overlaps, and the compatibility of the
279	  \(\phi_i\) with \((g_{ij}, h_{ij})\) is precisely the agreement condition,
280	  giving the unique glued \(\phi\). This specialises the general descent of sheaves
281	  of modules to the concrete open cover supplied by a scheme glue datum,
282	  and is the form in which the Grassmannian's tautological bundle and quotient are
283	  built.

286	  Grassmannian glue datum (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}), with glued scheme

288	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\). The glued sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\) is built from
289	  the per-chart sheaves \(\mathcal{M}_i\) and the overlap transition isomorphisms
290	  \(g_{ij}\) satisfying the module cocycle conditions, and is pinned down by the
291	  restriction isomorphisms \(\rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim}
292	  \mathcal{M}_i\) compatible with the \(g_{ij}\). For an open immersion
293	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\), the pullback \(\iota_i^{*}\) restricts sheaves
294	  of modules on \(X\) to sheaves of modules on \(U_i\). The transition isomorphisms
295	  \(g_{ij}\), forming a cocycle for this cover, constitute a descent datum; gluing the
296	  local sections yields \(\mathcal{M}\) together with the restriction isomorphisms
297	  \(\rho_i\), where both the assembly and the characterisation up to unique isomorphism
298	  rest on the locality of sections of a sheaf -- a compatible family of sections over
299	  the cover, agreeing on the overlaps, extends to a unique global section.
300	
301	  The morphism-descent half is the same locality argument applied to homomorphisms:
302	  a family \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\) commuting with the transition
303	  isomorphisms over every overlap is a compatible family of local morphisms, and the
304	  uniqueness of gluing of sections promotes it to a unique global
305	  \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) of sheaves of modules on \(X\) restricting
306	  to each \(\phi_i\) through the \(\rho_i\).
307	\end{definition}

\end{definition}

Conditions (C1) and (C2) are exactly the descent datum for the Zariski cover
  \(\{\iota_i\}\); it is subject to them that the gluing is well-defined. Out of this
  data the construction produces a glued sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
  \(\mathcal{M}\) on the glued scheme \(X\).

  \emph{Construction.} The glued sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\) is assembled in three steps,
  each a separate sub-result: its underlying presheaf is the presheaf of compatible
  families of chart sections \cref{def:gr_modules_gluePresheaf}; this presheaf carries
  a pointwise \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module structure \cref{def:gr_modules_gluePresheafModule};
  and it satisfies the sheaf condition \cref{lem:gr_modules_gluePresheaf_isSheaf}, the
  amalgamation being computed componentwise from the chart sheaf conditions. Packaging
  the resulting sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is the object \(\mathcal{M} =
  D.\mathrm{glued}.\mathrm{Modules}\) returned here. This specialises effective descent
  of sheaves of modules to the concrete open cover \(\{\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\}\)
  supplied by a scheme glue datum, and is the form in which the Grassmannian's
  tautological bundle and quotient are built.

  The glued sheaf comes with three further structures, each recorded as its own block
  downstream: the \emph{restriction isomorphisms} \(\rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}
  \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}_i\) compatible with the \(g_{ij}\) over the overlaps
  \cref{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso}; the \emph{characterisation up to unique
  isomorphism} by the restriction property \cref{lem:gr_modules_glue_unique}; and the
  \emph{descent of morphisms} -- a family \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\)
  commuting with the transition isomorphisms glues to a unique \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to
  \mathcal{N}\) with \(\rho_i^{\mathcal{N}} \circ \iota_i^{*}\phi = \phi_i \circ
  \rho_i^{\mathcal{M}}\) \cref{def:gr_modules_glueHom}.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Restriction isomorphisms of the glued sheaf]
  \label{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueRestrictionIso}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_glue, lem:over_restrict_pullback_iso,
    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport, lem:gr_glueData_bridges}
  Let \(\mathcal{M}\) be the glued sheaf of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}. For each
  chart index \(i\) there is a \emph{restriction isomorphism} of sheaves of
  \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules
  \[
    \rho_i \;:\; \iota_i^{*}\,\mathcal{M} \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{M}_i,
  \]
  compatible with the transition isomorphisms: over each overlap \(V_{ij}\) the two
  restrictions \(\rho_i\) and \(\rho_j\), transported to the common overlap, differ
  exactly by \(g_{ij}\).
\end{definition}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:gr_modules_gluePresheaf, lem:over_restrict_pullback_iso,
    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport, lem:gr_glueData_bridges}
  By the compatible-family description \cref{def:gr_modules_gluePresheaf}, a section of
  \(\mathcal{M}\) over an open of the form \(\iota_i^{-1}V\), restricted to the chart
  \(U_i\), is recovered from and recovers its \(i\)-th component
  \(s_i \in \Gamma(\mathcal{M}_i, \cdot)\); the chart-restriction bridge
  \cref{lem:over_restrict_pullback_iso} identifies the pullback \(\iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}\)
  with this restriction, and the \(i\)-th projection \((s_\bullet) \mapsto s_i\) is the
  isomorphism \(\rho_i\) (with inverse extending a chart section by the overlap relation).
  The overlap compatibility is the defining relation of the compatible families: over
  \(V_{ij}\) the components \(s_i, s_j\) are related by \(g_{ij}\), which is precisely the
  statement that \(\rho_i\) and \(\rho_j\) differ by \(g_{ij}\) after transport to
  \(V_{ij}\); the triple-overlap coherence needed for this to be consistent is (C2),
  whose transports are aligned by \cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport} and
  \cref{lem:gr_glueData_bridges}.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Uniqueness of the glued sheaf up to canonical isomorphism]
  \label{lem:gr_modules_glue_unique}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glue_unique}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_glue, def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso,
    lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
  Suppose \(\mathcal{M}'\) is another sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules equipped with
  isomorphisms \(\rho'_i : \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}' \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}_i\)
  compatible with the \(g_{ij}\) over the overlaps. Then there is a unique isomorphism
  \(\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}'\) intertwining the restriction
  isomorphisms, i.e.\ with \(\rho'_i \circ \iota_i^{*}(\cdot) = \rho_i\) for every \(i\).
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso, lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
  A morphism of sheaves of modules on \(X\) is determined by, and may be assembled from,
  its restrictions to the members of the open cover \(\{\iota_i\}\), agreeing on the
  overlaps -- the locality and gluing of sections
  (\cref{lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}) applied to the internal hom. The
  intertwining condition forces the restriction over \(U_i\) to be
  \((\rho'_i)^{-1} \circ \rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M} \to \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}'\);
  these local isomorphisms agree on each overlap \(V_{ij}\) because both
  \((\rho_i)\) and \((\rho'_i)\) are compatible with the \emph{same} transition family
  \((g_{ij})\) (\cref{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso}). By locality they glue to a
  unique global isomorphism \(\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}'\) with the
  required property; uniqueness is the separation half of the same gluing.
\end{proof}

\begin{definition}[Descent of morphisms along the cover]
  \label{def:gr_modules_glueHom}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueHom}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_glue, def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso,
    lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
  Let \((\mathcal{M}_i, g_{ij})\) and \((\mathcal{N}_i, h_{ij})\) be two glued data
  over the same glue datum \(D\), with glued sheaves \(\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}\) and
  restriction isomorphisms \(\rho^{\mathcal{M}}_i, \rho^{\mathcal{N}}_i\). Given a family
  of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-linear morphisms \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\)
  commuting with the transition isomorphisms on every overlap (i.e.\ \(h_{ij}\) intertwines
  the pullbacks of \(\phi_i\) and \(\phi_j\) over \(V_{ij}\)), there is a unique morphism
  of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) with
  \[
    \rho^{\mathcal{N}}_i \circ \iota_i^{*}\phi \;=\; \phi_i \circ \rho^{\mathcal{M}}_i
    \qquad \text{for every } i.
  \]
\end{definition}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso, lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
  On each chart the prescribed identity defines a local morphism
  \((\rho^{\mathcal{N}}_i)^{-1} \circ \phi_i \circ \rho^{\mathcal{M}}_i :
  \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M} \to \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{N}\). The commutation of the \(\phi_i\)
  with the transition isomorphisms \((g_{ij}, h_{ij})\), together with the overlap
  compatibility of the \(\rho\)-families (\cref{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso}), makes
  these local morphisms agree on every overlap \(V_{ij}\). By the locality and gluing of
  sections (\cref{lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}) applied to the internal hom they
  promote to a unique global morphism \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) restricting
  to each \(\phi_i\) through the \(\rho_i\) -- the descent of \(\mathrm{Hom}\) along the
  cover.
\end{proof}
